items.front: EDST: what four data sources does it use to derive expected aircraft trajectories?
items.back: Current plan data, forecast winds, aircraft performance characteristics, and track data. Per 13-1-1.

items.front: EDST current plan vs pilot's actual clearance: key distinction
items.back: The current plan is what EAS predicts the aircraft WILL fly and may include clearances NOT YET ISSUED to the aircraft. It may diverge from what the pilot has actually been told. Per 13-1-1 NOTE.

items.front: Conflict probe: what two types of alerts must be actively scanned for?
items.back: Predicted aircraft-to-aircraft alerts AND aircraft-to-airspace alerts. Per 13-1-2(a).

items.front: Conflict probe: what does it NOT account for?
items.back: Instances where GREATER separation is needed (e.g., non-standard formations, A380) and where REDUCED separation is permitted (e.g., 3 mile airspace). It is based on approved radar separation only. Per 13-1-2 NOTE.

items.front: Stop Probe restart: when does the requirement NOT apply?
items.back: When the aircraft is entering a non-EDST facility. Otherwise, conflict probe must be restarted before transfer of control unless otherwise coordinated. Per 13-1-2(e) and NOTE.

items.front: Trial planning: the four things it evaluates
items.back: 1. Solutions to predicted conflicts. 2. Feasibility of granting user requests. 3. Feasibility of removing a flight direction constraint (inappropriate altitude for direction of flight). 4. Feasibility of removing a static restriction. Per 13-1-3(a-d).

items.front: Trial planning: what two preconditions must be met before use?
items.back: EDST must be operational at the sector AND sector priorities must permit. Both conditions are required. Per 13-1-3.

items.front: Trial plan shows no alerts for a user request: what is the rule?
items.back: Make EVERY EFFORT to grant the request, UNLESS the change is likely to adversely affect operations at another sector. Per 13-1-4.

items.front: Flight progress strips: when must they be posted?
items.back: 1. For any nonradar flights. 2. For any flight plan not contained in the EAS. 3. Any strips deemed necessary for safe or efficient operations. Per 13-1-5(f-h).

items.front: Special Posting Area (SPA): what is it used for?
items.back: Grouping aircraft that have special significance, such as aircraft to be sequenced, air refueling missions, and formations. Per 13-1-5(e).
